text: The surgeon opens and dissects the left side end-pelvic fascia cordially, performing a conventional nerve-sparing technique.
The surgeon preserves the end-pelvic fascia on the right side, maintaining the integrity of the fascia during the procedure.
The surgeon cuts the detrusor apron and divides the prostate from the surrounding tissue, separating it from adjacent structures.
The surgeon uses the third arm to grip the urethral catheter while dividing the posterior side.
The surgeon divides the retrotrigonal layer and grasps the bilateral vas deferens and seminal vesicles.
The surgeon begins the nerve-sparing steps, focusing on preserving the surrounding neural structures while continuing the dissection.
On the left side, the surgeon performs conventional nerve sparing, using Hemlock grips to manage the lateral pedicle vessels.
The surgeon performs nerve sparing on the right side, preserving the endoperific fascia during the procedure.
The surgeon cuts through the anterolateral superficial vascular layer to expose the surface of the prostate.
The surgeon applies hemlock clips to each cut to prevent bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the prostate from the posterior side, moving towards the right lateral plane and extending the dissection cordially to the apex.
At the apex, the surgeon strips the prostatic muscles, revealing the underlying tissue layers.
The surgeon ligates the deep dorsal venous complex and sutures it using 3-0 Monocryl, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon cuts the urethra and removes the prostate, completing the excision of the gland.
The prostate bed is visible with the left side pelvic muscles exposed, while the right side remains covered by the end-pelvic fascia and vascular layers.
